title: NATO Ankara Summit Opens Amid Crackdown, Arms Deals, and F-35 Drama
subhead: Turkey hosts 32 NATO leaders for a two-day summit in Ankara, showcasing its defense industry and diplomatic clout — while rounding up critics, sparring with Israel over jet sales, and angling for European air defense systems.

sections.heading: NATO Ankara Summit Opens With Defense Spending and Arms Deals in Focus
sections.body:
The leaders of NATO's 32 member countries arrived in Ankara on Tuesday for a two-day summit focused on translating last year's defense spending pledges into concrete procurement deals. NATO allies plan to unveil arms deals worth 'tens of billions of dollars' on Tuesday, according to Reuters, as European capitals seek to demonstrate they are heeding U.S. demands for greater burden-sharing.

Under pressure from President Trump, NATO members committed last year to raise defense spending from 2% to 3.5% of GDP by 2035, with an additional 1.5% earmarked for defence-related expenditure. Trump branded allied spending as 'ridiculous' just days before the summit, according to Politico. Wednesday's session will focus on Europe taking greater responsibility for its own defense as the U.S. shifts focus to the Pacific.

The summit opens alongside a major Defense Industry Forum with roughly 3,500 companies showcasing hardware — a deliberate Turkish showcase of its booming defense sector, which has become a significant export industry.

Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky is due to join NATO leaders and hold a one-on-one meeting with Trump, pressing for €140 billion ($160 billion) in military aid pledges and renewed efforts to revive stalled peace talks with Russia, DPA reports. Russia's missile and drone attack on Kyiv overnight Monday, which killed at least 22 people according to CBC, adds urgency to those discussions.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey is using the summit to cement its position as a indispensable NATO power at a time when the alliance is under strain — and turning its defense industry boom into diplomatic leverage.

sections.heading: Turkey Rounds Up Critics Ahead of Summit as NATO's Rutte Raises Rights Concerns
sections.body:
Turkish authorities detained over 200 people in Ankara raids ahead of the summit, according to The Guardian, targeting opposition figures, journalists, and activists. A protest ban is in effect in Ankara until July 10. More than 100 anti-NATO protesters were detained separately, mostly from the Communist Party (TKP) and leftist groups, Outlook India reports. Two journalists were arrested on Sunday, and a Turkish court jailed comedian Deniz Goktas pending trial on charges of insulting religious values and President Erdogan, the Seattle Post-Intelligencer reports.

NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte stressed the right to demonstrate and media freedom in response to Turkey's pre-summit crackdown, MSN reports. The New York Times reported that Turkey is simultaneously cracking down on press freedom as NATO leaders gather — a tension that mirrors the broader contradiction of an authoritarian host showcasing democratic alliance values.

Meanwhile, the legal campaign against jailed Istanbul Mayor Ekrem Imamoglu is intensifying. An EU Parliament observer at the Imamoglu trial sent a message to NATO leaders about democratic backsliding, Indicator.gr reports. Imamoglu faces a 'triple hearing marathon' as the judicial squeeze tightens, MSN reports, and allegations of humiliation of co-defendants have drawn outrage.
sections.whyItMatters: The crackdown exposes the fundamental tension at the heart of the summit: Turkey is essential to NATO's military posture but is moving further from the alliance's democratic norms — and NATO's Rutte is pushing back publicly.

sections.heading: Netanyahu Pleads With Trump to Block F-35 Sales to Turkey
sections.body:
Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu publicly asked President Trump on Monday not to restore Turkey's access to F-35 stealth fighter jets, calling Turkey's government a 'regime infected by the Muslim Brotherhood' that 'chants death to America,' according to his Fox News interview reported by RealClearPolitics. Netanyahu warned that providing Ankara with advanced weapons would upset the regional balance of power, the Times of Israel reports.

Netanyahu also lamented the prospective sale of F110 fighter jet engines for Turkey's indigenous KAAN fighter, according to Jewish Insider. The Zionist Organization of America and ANCA have both pressed Congress to block arms sales to Turkey, citing its S-400 possession and hostility toward U.S. allies.

The New York Times reported that Trump is expected to tell Turkey he is ready to restore access to F-35 jets — a move that would reverse the ban he himself imposed in 2019 over Turkey's purchase of Russian S-400 systems. Israel and Greece conducted joint military drills in the Aegean ahead of the summit, Al-Monitor reports, in an apparent signal to Ankara.
sections.whyItMatters: The F-35 question puts Trump in a diplomatic bind between two allies — Israel and Turkey — and a decision either way reshapes the regional air power balance.

sections.heading: Turkey Courts Western Air Defense as S-400 Remains a White Elephant
sections.body:
Defense Minister Yasar Guler told Reuters in late June that Turkey is exploring 'all options' for air defense, specifically naming the American MIM-104 Patriot and the Franco-Italian SAMP/T system. Reuters reported Monday that France is now open to a possible SAMP/T sale to Turkey after years of political opposition — a significant shift driven by the post-Iran war defense calculus and Turkey's NATO hosting role.

President Erdogan is expected to push French President Macron directly on the SAMP/T during the summit, according to Turkish officials cited by MSN. Turkey's 2019 acquisition of Russian S-400 systems got it kicked out of the F-35 program and never yielded technology transfer or co-production benefits. The S-400s remain in storage, were never activated, and had no role during the Iran war when Turkey depended on NATO-deployed Patriots and SM-3 interceptors for anti-missile protection.

Turkey is now developing its indigenous Siper Block-III missile for anti-ballistic capability, while seeking Patriot or SAMP/T as interim solutions. Turkey has also begun constructing its Steel Dome national integrated air defense system, deliberately excluding the S-400.
sections.whyItMatters: The S-400 saga is ending not with integration but with irrelevance — and Turkey is pivoting hard toward Western systems, with France's软化stance a direct dividend of the summit moment.

sections.heading: Strait of Hormuz Reopening and the Energy Picture for Turkey
sections.body:
Ships are moving through the Persian Gulf again as the U.S.-Iran framework deal takes hold, the New York Times reports, and OPEC+ has pledged to pump more crude — putting downward pressure on energy prices. Iran and Oman are negotiating a pay model for the Strait of Hormuz, El Pais reports, with the White House opposing any fee on shipping while leveraging the prospect of lifting sanctions on Iranian oil.

For Turkey, which imports nearly all its oil and gas, the easing of Hormuz disruptions is significant. The Central Bank held its benchmark rate at 37% in June for a third consecutive meeting, monitoring the inflationary impact of the Iran war, Reuters reports. Turkey's manufacturing PMI fell to 45.7 in April before rebounding to 49.8 in May as supply chain disruptions from the war eased, Crypto Briefing reports. Turkey ranks fourth globally in food inflation at 34.55%, according to Trading Economics data cited by Bianet.

The Economist reported that Turkey is positioning itself to 'win from the Iran war' economically, leveraging its defense exports and geographic position as an alternative energy transit corridor to Hormuz-dependent routes.
sections.whyItMatters: Hormuz reopening eases Turkey's energy import bill and inflation pressure — but the central bank's 37% rate and 34% food inflation show how far recovery still has to go.

sections.heading: Turkey Leverages Defense Industry and Strategic Position for European Integration
sections.body:
Kurdistan24 reports that Turkey is seeking a deeper strategic role in European defense, leveraging its booming defense sector and growing military clout to secure deeper integration into Europe's defense architecture. The NATO summit and its 3,500-company Defense Industry Forum are designed to make that case tangible.

Anadolu Agency quoted Germany's Deputy Defense Minister saying the Ankara summit will 'show Trump that Europe is stepping up' — a line that reflects how Turkey is positioning itself as the bridge between European defense ambitions and American demands for burden-sharing. Turkey's defense exports have surged in recent years, with armed drones (Bayraktar TB2/TB3) now operated by over 30 countries.

The New York Times noted that with the Ukraine war ongoing and Trump cooling on the alliance, NATO members are finding new appreciation for what Turkey brings — a large military, strategic location bordering Russia, Iran, and Syria, and a defense industry that can produce at scale. Euro2day.gr observed that the summit finds 'revisionist Turkey strategically useful and ever-consistent Greece in a difficult position.'
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey is turning its geographic and military weight into a credible bid for deeper European defense integration — a shift that could reshape NATO's southern flank if institutional barriers follow.

bottomLine: The NATO Ankara summit opened Tuesday with Turkey at the center of multiple intersecting dramas: showcasing defense industry heft, pursuing Western air defense systems, fending off Netanyahu's F-35 blockade, and rounding up domestic critics even as NATO's Rutte publicly raised rights concerns. The summit's core business — converting last year's 3.5% GDP defense spending pledge into tens of billions in procurement deals — gives Turkey a chance to prove its indispensability to the alliance. But the contrast between Turkey's summit choreography and its domestic crackdown remains the story's sharpest edge.
sources: Reuters, AP, The Guardian, New York Times, Anadolu Agency, Politico, DPA, MSN, Bloomberg, Kurdistan24, The Media Line, Yahoo News, Jewish Insider, Times of Israel, Al-Monitor, El Pais, Bianet, Crypto Briefing, Economist, Euro2day.gr, Indicator.gr, Seattle Post-Intelligencer, Outlook India, RealClearPolitics, CBC, France24, RFE/RL
